slides.title: Introduction to C# Programming
slides.subtitle:
Modern, Powerful, Versatile Language
From Basics to Advanced Concepts

list.text: Enterprise Power: Developed by Microsoft and led by Anders Hejlsberg.
Multi-Paradigm: Supports object-oriented, functional, and generic programming.
Platform Neutral: Runs on Windows, Linux, and macOS via .NET.

box.title: C# (pronounced 'C-Sharp') is a modern, object-oriented, and type-safe programming language. It belongs to the C-family of languages (like C, C++, and Java) and will feel immediately familiar to many developers. C# was designed from the ground up to be simple, powerful, and productive, making it the primary language for the .NET ecosystem.

note.text: Released in 2002 alongside .NET Framework 1.0. Now a top choice for cloud, web, and enterprise apps.

list.text: Unified Platform: One runtime for Web, Mobile, Desktop, and Cloud.
CLR: The Common Language Runtime manages memory and execution.
NuGet: A massive ecosystem of over 300,000 packages.

box.title: .NET is a free, cross-platform, open-source developer platform for building many different types of applications. It provides the runtime, libraries, and tools needed to execute C# code efficiently. The ecosystem has evolved from the Windows-only '.NET Framework' to the modern, high-performance '.NET' (formerly .NET Core).

This is the classic structure of a C# program. It uses namespaces to organize code, classes to encapsulate logic, and a static Main method as the entry point. Every line here has a specific purpose in the C# compilation process.

box.code:
// C# 9.0 and above
Console.WriteLine("Hello, World!");
box.title: C# 9+ simplified approach

note.text: 💡 Top-level statements eliminate boilerplate code

box.title: Choosing the right numeric type is critical for performance and accuracy. C# distinguishes between integers, floating-point numbers, and decimal for financial accuracy. Understanding these ranges prevents overflow and precision errors.

C# offers several ways to work with text. String interpolation ($"") is the modern standard for concatenation, while verbatim strings (@"") and raw string literals (""" """) handle paths and multi-line text with ease.

C# provides multiple ways to declare variables. While explicit typing is traditional, modern C# heavily uses var for local variables to keep code clean without losing type safety. Once assigned, the type of a var variable is locked and cannot change.

Conditional statements allow your code to take different paths based on Boolean logic. C# supports standard if/else constructs, along with the 'ternary operator' for simple inline decisions. Modern C# also emphasizes patterns that reduce nested logic.

While the traditional switch statement is still available, modern C# (8.0+) introduces 'Switch Expressions'. They are more concise, return a value directly, and use 'Pattern Matching' to handle complex object states with minimal boilerplate.
Loops are fundamental for processing collections or repeating tasks. C# provides for for index-based iterating, and the powerful foreach for safe, readable iteration over any enumerable collection.

list.text: Encapsulation: Hiding internal state and requiring all interaction through a well-defined interface.
Inheritance: Allowing a class to inherit members from a base class, promoting code reuse.
Polymorphism: Enabling objects of different types to be treated as a common base type.
Abstraction: Modeling only the relevant data and behaviors of an object for the current context.

box.title: C# is a pure object-oriented language. To master it, you must understand the four core pillars that guide its design. These principles allow you to build software that is modular, reusable, and easy to maintain as it grows in complexity.

Modern C# has refined how we define property mutability. init properties allow setting values during initialization but make them read-only afterwards. The required keyword ensures that specific properties are always provided by the caller.

Inheritance allows a 'Derived' class to reuse and specialize the logic of a 'Base' class. Use the virtual keyword in the base class to allow methods to be overridden in derived classes, enabling specialized behavior while maintaining a common interface.

Interfaces define a 'contract' that implementing classes must follow. They contain method signatures but no implementation (until C# 8.0). Interfaces are the key to 'Loose Coupling' and are primary tools for building testable, modular systems.

list.text: List<T>: The workhorse dynamic array for general-purpose lists.
HashSet<T>: Ensures unique elements with O(1) performance.

box.title: C# offers a rich set of generic collections in the System.Collections.Generic namespace. These provide type-safe, high-performance data structures that are optimized for various use cases like rapid lookups or ordered processing.

LINQ (Language Integrated Query) is one of C#'s most powerful features. It allows you to query collections using a SQL-like syntax or functional method chains. It's fully type-safe and works with objects, databases (EF), and XML.

Modern C# applications depend on async/await for high performance and responsiveness. By using asynchronous patterns, you can perform I/O-bound tasks (like database queries or API calls) without blocking the main execution thread.

C# uses try-catch-finally blocks for structured exception handling. It's best practice to catch specific exceptions rather than the base Exception class, and to use finally for critical resource cleanup.

Delegates are type-safe function pointers, and Events are a way for a class to provide notifications to clients of that class when something of interest happens. This decoupling is fundamental to designing large-scale, extensible systems in .NET.

NuGet is the package manager for .NET. It allows you to easily share and consume code. With over 300,000 packages, it's the heart of the .NET ecosystem, providing libraries for everything from JSON parsing (Newtonsoft.Json) to robust logging (Serilog).

C# Records are ideal for data-centric objects. They provide built-in functionality for value-based equality and non-destructive mutation using the with expression. They are concise, thread-safe, and highly readable.

Pattern matching has evolved into a powerful tool for inspecting object shapes. Beyond simple type checks, you can now use Property Patterns and Positional Patterns to write expressive, safe conditional logic.

box.code:
#nullable enable
string name; // Cannot be null
string? nickname; // Can be null

Console.WriteLine(name.Length); // Safe
Console.WriteLine(nickname?.Length); // Safe (using null-conditional)
box.title: Nullable Reference Types (NRT) help eliminate the dreaded NullReferenceException. By enabling this feature, the compiler tracks nullability and warns you if you might be dereferencing a null value.

C# 12 introduces 'Primary Constructors' for all classes and structs, not just records. This feature reduces boilerplate by allowing you to declare parameters directly in the class header and use them throughout the class members.
Testing C# code

The Single Responsibility Principle (SRP) states that a class should have one, and only one, reason to change. In C#, this means keeping your classes focused and avoiding 'God Classes' that handle everything from data access to UI logic.

Classes should be open for extension but closed for modification. Use inheritance or interfaces to add new functionality without changing existing, tested code. This is the foundation of a stable and extensible codebase.

Objects of a superclass should be replaceable with objects of its subclasses without breaking the application. In C#, if you find yourself throwing NotImplementedException in a subclass method, you might be violating LSP.
Clients should not be forced to depend on methods they do not use. Instead of one fat interface, create many small, specific interfaces. This keeps your implementations clean and focused.
Depend on abstractions, not on concretions. High-level modules should not depend on low-level modules; both should depend on interfaces. This makes your system loosely coupled and highly testable.
For unmanaged resources (file handles, database connections), C# uses the IDisposable interface. The using statement ensures that resources are automatically cleaned up even if an exception occurs.

Entity Framework (EF) Core is the recommended Object-Relational Mapper (ORM) for .NET. It allows you to work with a database using C# objects, eliminating the need for most of the data-access code that you usually need to write.

list.text: C# in Depth by Jon Skeet - Deep dive book
Strong OOP support with modern features
Excellent for web, desktop, mobile, cloud, gaming
High demand in enterprise and startups

💻 Ready to code in C#?
Start building amazing applications today!

box.title: Span<T> is a stack-allocated representation of contiguous memory. It allows for high-performance slicing and manipulation of arrays, strings, and unmanaged memory without additional allocations, significantly reducing GC pressure.

Reflection allows you to inspect and interact with code metadata at runtime. While powerful for building frameworks, DI containers, and serializers, it should be used judiciously due to the performance overhead of dynamic lookups.
